blurb: Fragrant pink wild rose with red hips that hang on all winter.
seasons: Pink fragrant flowers May–Jul; red hips & canes persist through winter
wildlife: Xerces native/bumble bee value; hips feed birds & mammals
deer: Young spring leaves browsed — only somewhat resistant

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3–8 — among the hardiest wild roses in North America. Native from the plains to above 11,000 ft elevation, it shrugs off Front Range winters with no protection. Canes may die back to the snowline in a brutal winter but the root system is indestructible and regrows vigorously.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun for best flowering and densest growth. It tolerates light to moderate shade — woodland-edge plants bloom adequately — but open, sunny exposures give the most flowers and hips.[1][2]
care.soil: Adaptable and tough: tolerates sandy to clay-loam soils (pH ~5.6–7.0), and Front Range clay-loam without amendment. Prefers moderately fertile, well-drained to moist soil. Avoid permanently waterlogged spots and strongly alkaline sites. No special soil prep is needed for a native planting; compost incorporation helps on very heavy clay.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate once established — genuinely drought-tolerant as befits a plant native to dry foothills and prairie edges. Water deeply once a week during the first growing season to help roots establish. After that, watering every 2–3 weeks during summer heat is sufficient; on very dry years a monthly deep soak keeps it blooming and hip-bearing. Avoid frequent shallow watering and overhead irrigation, which promotes fungal disease.[1]
care.spacing: Plant 4–6 ft apart if you want the shrubs to grow together into a hedge or wildlife thicket; 6–8 ft apart for individual specimens. Mature plants reach 3–6 ft tall (occasionally to 10 ft in sheltered spots) and spread wider via root suckers. Site it where spreading is welcome, or plan to manage the perimeter.[1][2]
care.bloom: Fragrant, single, 5-petaled pink flowers (1–2 in across) open May through July on the Front Range — peak bloom is typically late May–June. Blooms form on the previous year's wood. Bright orange-red hips follow and persist on the canes well into winter, providing wildlife food long after the leaves drop.[1][2]
care.feeding: Minimal. Wood's rose thrives in lean native soils without supplemental fertilizer. A light topdress of compost in spring is fine; avoid high-nitrogen inputs, which encourage lush soft growth susceptible to disease and reduce flower and hip production.[1]
care.maintenance: Prune in late winter or early spring (February–March on the Front Range) before new growth breaks. Remove dead, damaged, and crossing canes at the base and thin congested older canes to improve air circulation — this is the key to disease prevention. Annual removal of a few of the oldest, woodiest canes keeps the shrub vigorous and blooming well; new canes from the base carry the best flowers. For a contained form, shorten long arching canes as needed; for a thicket or barrier, let it run and simply remove dead wood. Suckers at the colony margin can be spaded off each spring.[1]
care.selfsow: Spreads aggressively by rhizomes and root suckers, forming dense prickly thickets over time. This is excellent for wildlife habitat, erosion control, and property-edge barriers; manage the perimeter with a sharp spade annually to keep it from colonizing adjacent areas.[1][2]
care.troubles: Fairly robust compared to garden roses. Black spot (dark leaf spots, defoliation) and rust (orange pustules on leaf undersides) can appear in wet years — prevent both by watering at the base, pruning for airflow, and cleaning up fallen leaves. Aphids cluster on soft new growth in spring; a blast of water or insecticidal soap handles them. Spider mites may appear in hot, dry summers. Rose stem girdler (a native borer) occasionally kills individual canes — prune them out. Overall, far less disease-prone than hybrid garden roses.[1]
care.harvest: Hips are ready to harvest after the first frost softens them, typically October–November. They are high in vitamin C and make good tea, jelly, and syrup — slice off the blossom and stem ends, remove the seeds and fibrous interior, and cook. Leave at least half the hips on the shrub for overwintering birds and small mammals.[2][3]
care.companions: Chokecherry, mountain alder, red-twig dogwood, native willows, golden currant, rabbitbrush, and blue grama grass for a naturalistic Front Range hedgerow or wildlife planting. The prickly thicket it forms is excellent nesting habitat and complements open-meadow plantings of native grasses and prairie perennials.[1]
care.planting: Spring planting (April–May) gives the best establishment — bare-root plants transplanted while dormant root in quickly, and container stock planted before summer heat establishes with minimal stress. Fall planting (September) also works but is less reliable: research on Rosa woodsii bare-root stock shows markedly better 4-year survival from spring planting (93%) than fall (62%), so lean toward spring when you have the choice. In a container, Wood's rose grows willingly through the season and its roots handle freezing well (zone 3 hardy), but a pot left above ground on the Front Range will see roots freeze and thaw repeatedly — use a large container (≥10 gal), protect it from desiccating wind, or sink the pot into the ground over winter. Plant it out the following spring for long-term best results.[1][2]
care.propagation: Softwood cuttings taken in early summer with a heel and treated with rooting hormone are the most reliable vegetative method — published success rates of 92–99% at season's end. Division of rooted suckers in early spring is the easiest low-tech approach: sever a sucker well away from the parent crown with a sharp spade, dig it out with as much root as possible, and replant immediately. Seed requires stratification: soak the hip pulp off, then either cold-dry stratify at 32–50°F for 30+ days or do a two-step warm (68°F, 60 days) followed by cold (37°F, 90 days) treatment before spring sowing. The species forms a persistent seed bank (seeds viable 16+ years), so direct fall sowing in a prepared bed and waiting for natural stratification is a practical option for a wildlife planting. Cultivated starts from a native plant nursery are the fastest route to an established hedgerow.[1][2]

edible.card: Hips & petals (clean the hips' seed hairs)
edible.summary: The bright orange-red hips and the petals are edible and a well-documented native food, but the irritant seed hairs inside the hips must be removed before eating.[2][3]
edible.parts: Two parts are eaten: the fleshy outer rind of the hips (the ripe fruit) and the fragrant petals. Wood's-rose hips are a traditional Plains and Intermountain food — eaten fresh, dried, or cooked into jelly, syrup, and tea — and are rich in vitamin C; the petals make a fragrant garnish, tea, or flavoring. The seeds and the hairs surrounding them inside the hip are not eaten.[2][3]
edible.preparation: Harvest hips after the first frost softens and sweetens them. To use the rind safely, slice the hip open and scrape out the seeds and the bristly internal hairs, then cook the remaining flesh into jelly, syrup, or tea — or dry it. Straining cooked hip pulp through fine cloth is the reliable way to leave the irritant hairs behind. Petals can be used fresh. Avoid eating whole raw hips with the seed mass intact.[2][3]
edible.caution: The fine hairs surrounding the seeds inside each hip are a mechanical irritant — they can irritate the mouth, throat, and digestive tract (the same property that makes them a folk 'itching powder'), so always remove the seeds and strain out the hairs before eating. Rose hips and petals themselves are not poisonous, but be certain of identification: eat only hips you can confirm are a true rose (Rosa), and never eat material from plants treated with pesticides. The thorny canes are a physical hazard, not a toxic one.[2][3]
